title: Check if tree is complete
description: Determine whether a binary tree is a complete binary tree

examples.explanation: Queue: [1], nullFound=false. Dequeue 1: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue 2,3 → queue=[2,3]. Dequeue 2: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue 4,5 → queue=[3,4,5]. Dequeue 3: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue 6,null → queue=[4,5,6,null]. Dequeue 4: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue null,null → queue=[5,6,null,null,null]. Dequeue 5: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue null,null → queue=[6,null,null,null,null,null]. Dequeue 6: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue null,null → queue=[null,null,null,null,null,null,null]. Dequeue null: set nullFound=true. All remaining are null, no non-null after nullFound, return true.

Queue: [1], nullFound=false. Dequeue 1: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue 2,3 → queue=[2,3]. Dequeue 2: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue 4,5 → queue=[3,4,5]. Dequeue 3: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue null,7 → queue=[4,5,null,7]. Dequeue 4: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue null,null → queue=[5,null,7,null,null]. Dequeue 5: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue null,null → queue=[null,7,null,null,null,null]. Dequeue null: set nullFound=true. Dequeue 7: non-null, but nullFound=true → return false (gap detected).

approaches.name: Approach 1: Level Order Traversal with Null Detection
approaches.summary: Perform a level-order traversal (BFS) of the tree using a queue. Track when the first null node is encountered; after that, all subsequent nodes must also be null for the tree to be complete. This leverages the property that in a complete binary tree, nodes are filled from left to right without gaps.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Complete Binary Tree Check via Level Order Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement the complete solution to determine if a binary tree is complete using BFS with null detection.
ladders.explanation: This solution leverages the fundamental property of complete binary trees: nodes are filled from left to right without gaps. The intuition is that during a level-order traversal (BFS), once we encounter the first null node, all subsequent nodes in the queue must also be null. If we find any non-null node after the first null, the tree is incomplete because there's a gap in the level filling. The algorithm uses a queue to process nodes level by level. We enqueue the root and begin traversal. A boolean flag 'nullFound' is initialized to false. For each node dequeued: if it's null, we set nullFound to true; if it's non-null and nullFound is already true, we return false immediately (gap detected). Otherwise, we enqueue its left and right children (even if null) to maintain the level order. After processing all nodes, if no gap is found, return true. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(n) for the queue in the worst case (last level). This directly implements Approach 1. Common mistakes include not enqueuing null children (which breaks the detection), or incorrectly handling the root being null (a null tree is complete by definition). The pseudocode: function isComplete(root): if root is null: return true; queue = [root]; nullFound = false; while queue not empty: node = queue.dequeue(); if node is null: nullFound = true; else: if nullFound: return false; queue.enqueue(node.left); queue.enqueue(node.right); return true.

examples.trace: Queue: [1], nullFound=false. Dequeue 1: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue 2,3 → queue=[2,3]. Dequeue 2: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue 4,5 → queue=[3,4,5]. Dequeue 3: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue 6,null → queue=[4,5,6,null]. Dequeue 4: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue null,null → queue=[5,6,null,null,null]. Dequeue 5: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue null,null → queue=[6,null,null,null,null,null]. Dequeue 6: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue null,null → queue=[null,null,null,null,null,null,null]. Dequeue null: set nullFound=true. All remaining are null, no non-null after nullFound, return true.

Queue: [1], nullFound=false. Dequeue 1: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue 2,3 → queue=[2,3]. Dequeue 2: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue 4,5 → queue=[3,4,5]. Dequeue 3: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue null,7 → queue=[4,5,null,7]. Dequeue 4: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue null,null → queue=[5,null,7,null,null]. Dequeue 5: non-null, nullFound=false, enqueue null,null → queue=[null,7,null,null,null,null]. Dequeue null: set nullFound=true. Dequeue 7: non-null, but nullFound=true → return false (gap detected).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Null Node Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement the BFS traversal that processes nodes and tracks when the first null is encountered.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core of the full solution: performing a level-order traversal while monitoring for the first null node. The intuition is that in a complete tree, nulls should only appear at the end of the traversal; if a null appears and is followed by a non-null, it indicates a gap. We use a queue to ensure level-by-level processing. Start by enqueuing the root. Maintain a boolean flag 'nullSeen' initialized to false. While the queue is not empty: dequeue a node. If the node is null, set nullSeen to true. If the node is non-null: if nullSeen is true, we have detected a gap—this is where the logic would return false in the full problem, but here we just note it. Then, enqueue the left and right children (regardless of whether they are null) to continue the traversal. This ensures we process the tree in exact level order, including null positions. Time complexity is O(n) as we process each node once. Space complexity is O(n) for the queue. This feeds into L0 by providing the traversal framework; L0 adds the early return logic. Common mistakes include forgetting to enqueue null children (which would skip levels) or incorrectly updating the flag. Pseudocode: function bfsWithNullTracking(root): if root is null: return; queue = [root]; nullSeen = false; while queue not empty: node = queue.dequeue(); if node is null: nullSeen = true; print 'null'; else: if nullSeen: print 'gap detected'; print node.val; queue.enqueue(node.left); queue.enqueue(node.right).

examples.trace: Queue: [1], nullSeen=false. Dequeue 1: non-null, nullSeen=false, print 1, enqueue 2,3 → queue=[2,3]. Dequeue 2: non-null, nullSeen=false, print 2, enqueue null,null → queue=[3,null,null]. Dequeue 3: non-null, nullSeen=false, print 3, enqueue null,null → queue=[null,null,null,null]. Dequeue null: nullSeen=true, print 'null'. Dequeue null: nullSeen=true, print 'null'. Dequeue null: nullSeen=true, print 'null'. Done.

Queue: [1], nullSeen=false. Dequeue 1: non-null, nullSeen=false, print 1, enqueue null,3 → queue=[null,3]. Dequeue null: nullSeen=true, print 'null'. Dequeue 3: non-null, but nullSeen=true, print 'gap detected', print 3, enqueue null,null → queue=[null,null]. Dequeue null: nullSeen=true, print 'null'. Dequeue null: nullSeen=true, print 'null'. Done.

testCases.expected: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, null, null, null, null, null, null

ladders.title: Core Logic — Detecting Gaps After First Null in Sequence
ladders.desc: Implement the logic to check if any non-null appears after the first null in a sequence.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of Approach 1: in a complete binary tree, once a null node appears in level-order traversal, all following nodes must be null. The intuition stems from the left-to-right filling property: if there's a null (missing node), everything to its right in that level and below should also be missing. We process a sequence of nodes (e.g., from BFS) and track the first null occurrence. Initialize a flag 'firstNullSeen' to false. Iterate through each node in the sequence: if node is null and firstNullSeen is false, set firstNullSeen to true. If node is non-null and firstNullSeen is true, return false immediately (gap detected). Otherwise, continue. If we finish without gap, return true. Time complexity is O(k) for a sequence of length k. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing the condition checked during traversal. Common mistakes include not resetting the flag or misordering checks. Pseudocode: function hasGap(sequence): firstNullSeen = false; for node in sequence: if node is null: if not firstNullSeen: firstNullSeen = true; else: if firstNullSeen: return false; return true.

examples.trace: firstNullSeen=false. Node 1: non-null, firstNullSeen=false → continue. Node 2: non-null, firstNullSeen=false → continue. Node 3: non-null, firstNullSeen=false → continue. Node null: null, firstNullSeen=false → set firstNullSeen=true. Node null: null, firstNullSeen=true → continue. Node null: null, firstNullSeen=true → continue. End: no non-null after first null, return true.

firstNullSeen=false. Node 1: non-null, firstNullSeen=false → continue. Node null: null, firstNullSeen=false → set firstNullSeen=true. Node 3: non-null, but firstNullSeen=true → return false (gap detected).

ladders.title: Building Block — Level-order Traversal (BFS) of Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Implement BFS to traverse a binary tree level by level, including null nodes.
ladders.explanation: Level-order traversal, or Breadth-First Search (BFS), visits nodes of a tree level by level from top to bottom, left to right. The intuition is to use a queue to manage the order: start with the root, then repeatedly dequeue a node, process it, and enqueue its children. This ensures we complete one level before moving to the next. For complete tree checking, we must include null children in the queue to preserve positions. Algorithm: if root is null, return empty list. Initialize queue with root. While queue not empty: dequeue node. If node is null, record null; else, record node value, and enqueue left and right children (even if null). This yields a sequence representing the tree structure. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of nodes (including nulls enqueued). Space complexity is O(n) for the queue. This feeds into L2 by providing the sequence to check for gaps. Common mistakes include not handling null root or forgetting to enqueue nulls. Pseudocode: function levelOrderWithNulls(root): if root is null: return []; result = []; queue = [root]; while queue not empty: node = queue.dequeue(); if node is null: result.append(null); else: result.append(node.val); queue.enqueue(node.left); queue.enqueue(node.right); return result.

examples.trace: Queue: [1]. Dequeue 1: non-null, result=[1], enqueue 2,3 → queue=[2,3]. Dequeue 2: non-null, result=[1,2], enqueue null,null → queue=[3,null,null]. Dequeue 3: non-null, result=[1,2,3], enqueue null,null → queue=[null,null,null,null]. Dequeue null: result=[1,2,3,null]. Dequeue null: result=[1,2,3,null,null]. Dequeue null: result=[1,2,3,null,null,null]. Dequeue null: result=[1,2,3,null,null,null,null]. Queue empty, return.

Queue: [1]. Dequeue 1: non-null, result=[1], enqueue null,3 → queue=[null,3]. Dequeue null: result=[1,null]. Dequeue 3: non-null, result=[1,null,3], enqueue null,null → queue=[null,null]. Dequeue null: result=[1,null,3,null]. Dequeue null: result=[1,null,3,null,null]. Return.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Operations for BFS
ladders.desc: Implement and use basic queue operations (enqueue, dequeue) to manage node processing.
ladders.explanation: A queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure essential for BFS. The intuition is that we add nodes to the back and remove from the front, ensuring level-order processing. Basic operations: enqueue (add to rear) and dequeue (remove from front). In tree traversal, we start by enqueuing the root. Then, while the queue is not empty, we dequeue a node to process it, and enqueue its children to be processed later. This guarantees we visit nodes in the order they were discovered, i.e., by level. For implementation, we can use a list in Python (with append for enqueue and pop(0) for dequeue, though inefficient; better use collections.deque). Time complexity per operation is O(1) for deque. Space complexity is O(n) for storing nodes. This feeds into L3 as the mechanism to perform BFS. Common mistakes include using stack operations (LIFO) instead of queue, or not checking if queue is empty. Pseudocode: using list: queue = []; enqueue: queue.append(item); dequeue: if queue: return queue.pop(0); else: error. Using deque: from collections import deque; queue = deque(); enqueue: queue.append(item); dequeue: if queue: return queue.popleft(); else: error.

examples.input: Enqueue 1,2,3; dequeue twice; enqueue 4; dequeue thrice
examples.trace: Start queue: []. Enqueue 1 → queue=[1]. Enqueue 2 → queue=[1,2]. Enqueue 3 → queue=[1,2,3]. Dequeue → returns 1, queue=[2,3]. Dequeue → returns 2, queue=[3]. Enqueue 4 → queue=[3,4]. Dequeue → returns 3, queue=[4]. Dequeue → returns 4, queue=[]. Dequeue → error or None (queue empty).

examples.input: Enqueue null,5; dequeue; enqueue 6; dequeue twice
examples.trace: Start queue: []. Enqueue null → queue=[null]. Enqueue 5 → queue=[null,5]. Dequeue → returns null, queue=[5]. Enqueue 6 → queue=[5,6]. Dequeue → returns 5, queue=[6]. Dequeue → returns 6, queue=[]. Done.

testCases.expected: Error or None on second dequeue

testCases.input: Enqueue x; dequeue; enqueue y; dequeue; enqueue z; dequeue

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Null Values
ladders.desc: Understand and represent binary tree nodes, including null as missing nodes.
ladders.explanation: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. The foundation concept involves representing nodes and understanding null values. A node typically has attributes: value (data), left (reference to left child), and right (reference to right child). Null represents the absence of a child, i.e., no node exists in that position. In a complete binary tree, nodes are filled from left to right, so nulls only appear at the end of levels. Intuition: think of a tree as a family tree where each person can have up to two children; if a child is missing, it's null. For implementation, we define a class Node with value, left, right. We can build trees by linking nodes: e.g., root = Node(1); root.left = Node(2); root.right = Node(3); root.left.left = null. This feeds into L4 as we enqueue these nodes in BFS. Common mistakes include confusing null with a node having value null (it should be the absence, not a node). Pseudocode: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. To create tree [1,2,3]: root = Node(1); root.left = Node(2); root.right = Node(3); root.left.left = None; root.left.right = None; root.right.left = None; root.right.right = None.

examples.output: Single node with value 1, left=null, right=null
examples.trace: Create Node(1): val=1, left=None, right=None. That's the root. No children, so left and right are null.

examples.output: Root 1 with left child 2, right null
examples.trace: Create Node(1): root. Create Node(2): left child. Set root.left = Node(2). Set root.right = None. For Node(2), set left=None, right=None. Structure: root.val=1, left points to node with val=2, right=null.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Recursive Index and Count Validation
approaches.summary: Assign an index to each node starting from 1 for the root, and recursively compute the total number of nodes. A tree is complete if the maximum index equals the total node count, ensuring no gaps in the indexing. This method uses the mathematical relationship between node indices and tree size.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

ladders.title: Full Problem — Complete Binary Tree Check via Index-Count Validation
ladders.desc: Implement the complete solution to determine if a binary tree is complete using recursive index assignment and node counting.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages the mathematical property of complete binary trees: if we assign indices to nodes starting from 1 at the root (left child gets 2*parent_index, right child gets 2*parent_index+1), then in a complete tree, the maximum index equals the total number of nodes, indicating no gaps in the indexing. The algorithm works by performing two recursive traversals: first to count total nodes, then to find the maximum index. If max_index == total_nodes, the tree is complete. Intuition: In a complete binary tree, nodes fill levels left-to-right without missing any positions, which this index-count equivalence captures perfectly. Step-by-step: 1. Define a recursive function count_nodes(root) that returns 0 for null, else 1 + count_nodes(left) + count_nodes(right). 2. Define a recursive function max_index(root, index) that returns 0 for null, else max(index, max_index(left, 2*index), max_index(right, 2*index+1)). 3. In main function: total = count_nodes(root); if total == 0 return True; max_idx = max_index(root, 1); return max_idx == total. Time: O(n) for both traversals. Space: O(h) for recursion stack. Connection to L1: This integrates the key sub-routines of counting and index-finding. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle empty tree (should return True), incorrect index calculation (must start at 1), or not using proper base cases. Real code pattern: def is_complete(root): def count(node): return 0 if not node else 1 + count(node.left) + count(node.right); def max_idx(node, i): return 0 if not node else max(i, max_idx(node.left, 2*i), max_idx(node.right, 2*i+1)); total = count(root); return total == 0 or max_idx(root, 1) == total.

examples.input: Tree: root(1) with left=2, right=3; node2 left=4, right=5; node3 left=6, right=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Count nodes. count(1): 1 + count(2) + count(3). count(2): 1 + count(4) + count(5) = 1+1+1=3. count(4): 1+0+0=1. count(5): 1+0+0=1. count(3): 1 + count(6) + count(null) = 1+1+0=2. count(6): 1+0+0=1. Total = 1+3+2=6. Step 2: Find max index starting at index=1. max_idx(1,1): max(1, max_idx(2,2), max_idx(3,3)). max_idx(2,2): max(2, max_idx(4,4), max_idx(5,5)) = max(2,4,5)=5. max_idx(4,4): max(4,0,0)=4. max_idx(5,5): max(5,0,0)=5. max_idx(3,3): max(3, max_idx(6,6), max_idx(null,7)) = max(3,6,0)=6. max_idx(6,6): max(6,0,0)=6. Overall max = max(1,5,6)=6. Step 3: Compare: total=6, max_idx=6 → True.

examples.input: Tree: root(1) with left=2, right=3; node2 left=4, right=null; node3 left=null, right=5
examples.trace: Step 1: Count nodes. count(1): 1 + count(2) + count(3). count(2): 1 + count(4) + count(null) = 1+1+0=2. count(4): 1+0+0=1. count(3): 1 + count(null) + count(5) = 1+0+1=2. count(5): 1+0+0=1. Total = 1+2+2=5. Step 2: Find max index. max_idx(1,1): max(1, max_idx(2,2), max_idx(3,3)). max_idx(2,2): max(2, max_idx(4,4), max_idx(null,5)) = max(2,4,0)=4. max_idx(4,4): max(4,0,0)=4. max_idx(3,3): max(3, max_idx(null,6), max_idx(5,7)) = max(3,0,7)=7. max_idx(5,7): max(7,0,0)=7. Overall max = max(1,4,7)=7. Step 3: Compare: total=5, max_idx=7 → False (gap at index 6).

testCases.input: root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4 right=5; node3 left=6
root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4 right=5; node3 left=6 right=7
root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4 right=5; node3 left=null right=6
root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4 right=5; node3 left=6 right=null; node4 left=7
root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4 right=5; node3 left=6 right=7; node4 left=8

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Recursive Maximum Index Finder
ladders.desc: Implement the function that computes the maximum index assigned to any node in the tree using the indexing scheme.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is critical because it captures whether there are gaps in the tree structure. The intuition: In a complete binary tree, indices should be consecutive from 1 to total_nodes. If the maximum index exceeds total_nodes, it indicates missing nodes (gaps). The algorithm recursively traverses the tree, assigning indices based on parent-child relationships: left child index = 2*parent_index, right child index = 2*parent_index+1. For each node, we compute the maximum among its index and the maxima from its subtrees. Step-by-step: 1. Base case: if node is null, return 0 (no index). 2. Recursive case: return max(current_index, max_index(left_child, 2*current_index), max_index(right_child, 2*current_index+1)). Time: O(n) as we visit each node once. Space: O(h) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function provides the max_index needed for the final comparison. Common mistakes: Starting index not at 1, incorrect arithmetic for child indices, or not handling null properly. Real code pattern: def max_index(node, idx): if not node: return 0; return max(idx, max_index(node.left, 2*idx), max_index(node.right, 2*idx+1)).

examples.input: Tree: root(A) with left=B, right=C; start index=1
examples.trace: Step 1: max_index(A,1): max(1, max_index(B,2), max_index(C,3)). Step 2: max_index(B,2): B has no children → max(2, max_index(null,4), max_index(null,5)) = max(2,0,0)=2. Step 3: max_index(C,3): C has no children → max(3, max_index(null,6), max_index(null,7)) = max(3,0,0)=3. Step 4: Overall: max(1,2,3)=3.

examples.input: Tree: root(X) with left=Y; start index=1
examples.trace: Step 1: max_index(X,1): max(1, max_index(Y,2), max_index(null,3)). Step 2: max_index(Y,2): Y has no children → max(2, max_index(null,4), max_index(null,5)) = max(2,0,0)=2. Step 3: max_index(null,3): returns 0. Step 4: Overall: max(1,2,0)=2.

testCases.input: root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4, idx=1
root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4 right=5, idx=1
root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4 right=5; node3 left=6, idx=1
root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4 right=5; node3 left=6 right=7, idx=1
root(A) left=B right=C; B left=D, idx=10

ladders.title: Core Logic — Index-Count Equivalence for Completeness
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the mathematical insight that a binary tree is complete iff max_index == total_nodes.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight driving the approach. Intuition: In a complete binary tree, nodes are packed tightly without gaps across levels. By assigning indices starting from 1 at the root (with left child at 2*i, right at 2*i+1), we create a perfect mapping to an array representation. If the tree is complete, every index from 1 to total_nodes corresponds to a node, so max_index equals total_nodes. If not complete, there will be gaps (missing nodes), causing max_index to be greater than total_nodes. For example, a tree with a missing left child but a right child will have a jump in indices. Step-by-step: 1. Compute total_nodes via traversal. 2. Compute max_index via traversal with indexing. 3. Compare: if equal → complete, else not. Time: O(n) for each computation. Space: O(h). Connection to L1: This logic uses the max_index function as a component. Common mistakes: Misunderstanding the indexing scheme (must start at 1), or not considering edge cases like empty tree. Real code pattern: total = count_nodes(root); max_idx = max_index(root, 1); return total == max_idx.

examples.input: Tree with 3 nodes: root(1) left=2 right=3
examples.trace: Step 1: total_nodes = 3. Step 2: Indices: root=1, left=2, right=3 → max_index=3. Step 3: 3 == 3 → True.

examples.input: Tree: root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4
examples.trace: Step 1: total_nodes = 4. Step 2: Indices: root=1, left=2, right=3, node4=4 → but node3 has no left child, so index for potential left child of node3 is 6, but it's null, so max_index might be higher if there's a gap. Actually, with given nodes: indices are 1,2,3,4. max_index=4. Wait, need to compute properly: max_index(1,1): left child at 2 has left child at 4, right child at 3 has no children, so max_index = max(1, max(2,4,0), max(3,0,0)) = max(1,4,3)=4. Step 3: 4 == 4 → True? Correction: This tree is actually complete (nodes: 1,2,3,4 in order). Let's use a non-complete example: root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4 right=null; node3 left=null right=5. Total_nodes=5. Indices: 1,2,3,4,7 (since node5 is right child of node3 at index 7). max_index=7. 5 != 7 → False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal for Counting Nodes
ladders.desc: Implement a function to count the total number of nodes in a binary tree using recursion.
ladders.explanation: This is an intermediate concept needed for the approach. Intuition: To check completeness, we need the total number of nodes to compare with the maximum index. Counting nodes is straightforward: each node contributes 1, and we sum counts from left and right subtrees. Step-by-step: 1. Base case: if node is null, return 0. 2. Recursive case: return 1 + count_nodes(left_child) + count_nodes(right_child). This is a post-order traversal (process children before root). Time: O(n) as we visit each node once. Space: O(h) for recursion stack. Connection to L2: This provides the total_nodes for the equivalence check. Common mistakes: Forgetting base case, or incorrect addition. Real code pattern: def count_nodes(node): if not node: return 0; return 1 + count_nodes(node.left) + count_nodes(node.right).

examples.trace: Step 1: count(1): 1 + count(2) + count(3). Step 2: count(2): 1 + count(null) + count(null) = 1+0+0=1. Step 3: count(3): 1 + count(null) + count(null) = 1+0+0=1. Step 4: Total: 1+1+1=3.

examples.input: Tree: root(A) with left=B; B left=C
examples.trace: Step 1: count(A): 1 + count(B) + count(null). Step 2: count(B): 1 + count(C) + count(null). Step 3: count(C): 1 + count(null) + count(null) = 1. Step 4: count(B): 1+1+0=2. Step 5: count(A): 1+2+0=3.

testCases.input: root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4 right=5
root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4 right=5; node3 left=6
root(1) left=2 right=3; node2 left=4 right=5; node3 left=6 right=7
root(A) left=B right=C; B left=D right=E; C left=F
root(X) left=Y; Y left=Z; Z left=W

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Recursion
ladders.desc: Understand and use the basic binary tree node structure and simple recursive calls.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves defining a binary tree node and performing a recursive traversal. Intuition: Binary trees are hierarchical structures where each node has at most two children (left and right). Recursion is natural for trees because each subtree is itself a tree. Step-by-step: 1. Define a class Node with attributes: value, left, right. 2. Write a recursive function that takes a node as input. 3. Base case: if node is null, return some default value (e.g., 0). 4. Recursive case: perform operation on node, then call function on left and right children. Time: Depends on traversal, typically O(n). Space: O(h) for recursion. Connection to L3: This is the foundation for counting nodes or other traversals. Common mistakes: Not checking for null pointers, infinite recursion, or incorrect order of calls. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. def traverse(node): if not node: return; traverse(node.left); traverse(node.right).

examples.input: Tree: root(10) with left=20, right=30; perform a traversal that prints values
examples.trace: Step 1: Call traverse(10). Step 2: Since node not null, call traverse(left=20). Step 3: traverse(20): not null, call traverse(left=null) which returns, call traverse(right=null) returns, then print 20. Step 4: Back to traverse(10), call traverse(right=30). Step 5: traverse(30): similar, print 30. Step 6: Back to traverse(10), print 10. Output order: 20,30,10.

examples.input: Tree: root(5) with left=3; perform a function that returns sum of values
examples.trace: Step 1: Define sum_nodes(node): if not node: return 0; return node.val + sum_nodes(node.left) + sum_nodes(node.right). Step 2: sum_nodes(5): 5 + sum_nodes(3) + sum_nodes(null). Step 3: sum_nodes(3): 3 + sum_nodes(null) + sum_nodes(null) = 3. Step 4: sum_nodes(null): 0. Step 5: Total: 5+3+0=8.

testCases.input: root(10) left=20 right=30; node20 left=40, traverse
root(X) left=Y right=Z; Y left=W, sum_values

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Trees and Recursion Basics
ladders.desc: Learn the basic definitions of binary trees and simple recursive thinking.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept. Intuition: A binary tree is a data structure where each node has at most two children, referred to as left and right. It's used to represent hierarchical data. Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of the same problem. For trees, recursion works well because each child is a subtree. Step-by-step: 1. Define a binary tree: a set of nodes where each node contains a value and references to left and right children. 2. Understand null/empty tree: a tree with no nodes. 3. Recursive example: to compute the height of a tree, height(node) = 0 if null, else 1 + max(height(left), height(right)). Time: O(n) for full traversal. Space: O(h). Connection to L4: This foundation enables writing recursive functions on trees. Common mistakes: Confusing trees with other structures, or not grasping base cases in recursion. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. def example_recursion(node): if node is None: return 0; else: return 1 + example_recursion(node.left).

examples.input: What is the height of a tree with root only?
examples.trace: Step 1: Define height(node): if node is null, return 0; else return 1 + max(height(left), height(right)). Step 2: For root only: node not null, so height = 1 + max(height(null), height(null)) = 1 + max(0,0) = 1.

examples.input: Draw a binary tree with root A, left child B, and right child C.
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with node A as root. Step 2: Attach left child B to A. Step 3: Attach right child C to A. Step 4: B and C have no children (null). Visual: A / \ B C.

testCases.input: Define a binary tree node in Python.
testCases.expected: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None

testCases.input: What is the base case for recursion on trees?
How many children can a binary tree node have?
What is the height of an empty tree?
Create a tree with root value 10.
Recursive function to check if tree is empty: def is_empty(node): ...
What does a leaf node in a binary tree have?

testCases.expected: A function that calls itself to solve smaller problems

testCases.input: Draw a tree with root X and left child Y.

testCases.expected: Because trees have recursive structure: each child is a subtree

approaches.name: Approach 3: Two-Pass Height and Traversal Check
approaches.summary: First, compute the height of the tree. Then, perform a traversal to verify that all levels except possibly the last are fully filled, and nodes in the last level are as far left as possible. This explicitly separates height calculation from level validation.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Complete Binary Tree Check via Two-Pass Height and Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement the complete solution to determine if a binary tree is complete using height calculation followed by level validation.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the complete binary tree verification problem by separating it into two distinct phases: height computation and level validation. The intuition is that a complete binary tree has all levels fully filled except possibly the last, where nodes must be as far left as possible. By first computing the tree's height, we establish the expected structure. Then, we perform a level-order traversal to verify two conditions: (1) all levels before the last must be completely filled (2^level nodes), and (2) in the last level, nodes appear contiguously from the left without gaps. The two-pass separation makes the logic cleaner than trying to validate everything in a single traversal. The algorithm begins by computing the height recursively: height = 1 + max(left_height, right_height). Then, we perform a BFS traversal, tracking the current level and node count. For each level except the last, we verify that the node count equals 2^level. For the last level, we ensure that once we encounter a null child, all subsequent nodes in that level must also be null (no gaps). Time complexity is O(n) since we visit each node twice (once for height, once for validation). Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack in height computation and O(w) for BFS queue, where w is maximum width (2^(h-1)), but O(h) dominates. This connects to L1 by using the height computation as a prerequisite. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle empty trees (height 0 should return true), incorrect base cases in height recursion, or not properly checking the 'no gaps' condition in the last level. Real code pattern: def is_complete(root): if not root: return True; height = compute_height(root); queue = deque([root]); level = 0; nodes_in_level = 0; expected_nodes = 1; last_level = False; while queue: node = queue.popleft(); if node: nodes_in_level += 1; if not last_level: queue.append(node.left); queue.append(node.right); if nodes_in_level == expected_nodes: if level == height-1: last_level = True; level += 1; expected_nodes *= 2; nodes_in_level = 0; else: if not last_level: return False; else: if last_level: for remaining in queue: if remaining: return False; break; return nodes_in_level == 0 or last_level.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute height. Root=1, left height from 2: node 2 has left 4 (height 1), right 5 (height 1), so height = 1+max(1,1)=2. Right height from 3: node 3 has left 6 (height 1), right null (height 0), so height = 1+max(1,0)=2. Overall height = 1+max(2,2)=3. Step 2: BFS validation. Level 0: expected nodes=1, queue=[1], process 1 (non-null), nodes_in_level=1, add children 2,3. Level complete, level=1, expected_nodes=2. Level 1: queue=[2,3], process 2 (non-null), nodes_in_level=1, add children 4,5; process 3 (non-null), nodes_in_level=2, add children 6,null. Level complete, level=2, expected_nodes=4, last_level set true. Level 2: queue=[4,5,6,null], process 4 (non-null), nodes_in_level=1; process 5 (non-null), nodes_in_level=2; process 6 (non-null), nodes_in_level=3; process null, since last_level and null encountered, check remaining queue empty, so valid. Return true.

Step 1: Compute height. Root=1, left height from 2: node 2 has left 4 (height 1), right 5 (height 1), height=2. Right height from 3: node 3 has left null (height 0), right 7 (height 1), height=2. Overall height=3. Step 2: BFS validation. Level 0: expected nodes=1, queue=[1], process 1, nodes_in_level=1, add 2,3. Level complete, level=1, expected_nodes=2. Level 1: queue=[2,3], process 2, nodes_in_level=1, add 4,5; process 3, nodes_in_level=2, add null,7. Level complete, level=2, expected_nodes=4, last_level true. Level 2: queue=[4,5,null,7], process 4 (non-null), nodes_in_level=1; process 5 (non-null), nodes_in_level=2; process null, since last_level and null encountered, but remaining queue has 7 (non-null), which violates 'no gaps' rule. Return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Height of Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Implement the height computation function used as the first pass in the complete tree check.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes the height (maximum depth) of a binary tree, which is essential for the two-pass approach. The intuition is that height defines the number of levels in the tree, and for a complete binary tree, we need to know the total levels to validate level filling. Height is defined as the number of edges on the longest path from root to a leaf. For an empty tree, height is 0 (or -1 depending on definition, but we use 0 for consistency with level counting). The algorithm uses recursion: height(node) = 0 if node is null, else 1 + max(height(node.left), height(node.right)). This recursively computes the height of left and right subtrees and takes the maximum, adding 1 for the current node. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(h) for the recursion stack, where h is the height. This connects to L0 by providing the height value needed for level validation. Common mistakes include: returning -1 for empty tree (which complicates level math), infinite recursion if base case missing, or not using max leading to incorrect height. Real code pattern: def height(root): if not root: return 0; return 1 + max(height(root.left), height(root.right)).

examples.trace: Root=1, not null. Compute left height: node 2, not null, left null (height 0), right null (height 0), so height(2)=1+max(0,0)=1. Compute right height: node 3, not null, left null (0), right null (0), height(3)=1. Overall height=1+max(1,1)=2.

Root=1, not null. Left height: node 2, not null, left: node 3, not null, left null (0), right null (0), so height(3)=1, height(2)=1+max(1,0)=2. Right height: null, height 0. Overall height=1+max(2,0)=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Level-order Traversal with Level Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement BFS traversal that tracks current level and node count per level.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves performing a level-order traversal (BFS) while keeping track of which level we're processing and how many nodes we've seen in that level. The intuition is that to validate a complete tree, we need to inspect nodes level by level and ensure each level (except last) is fully filled. BFS is ideal because it visits nodes in level order. We use a queue to store nodes, and maintain variables: current level, nodes counted in current level, and expected nodes for the level (which doubles each level: 1,2,4,...). The algorithm: initialize queue with root, level=0, nodes_in_level=0, expected=1. While queue not empty: dequeue node; if node non-null, increment nodes_in_level, enqueue its children; if nodes_in_level equals expected, check if this is the last level (level == height-1), if so set flag, else reset counters for next level. This ensures we process exactly the expected nodes per level. Time O(n), space O(w) for queue width. Connects to L0 by providing the traversal framework for validation. Common mistakes: not resetting counters after level complete, incorrect expected node calculation, or mishandling null nodes in queue. Real code pattern: queue=deque([root]); level=0; count=0; expected=1; last_level=False; while queue: node=queue.popleft(); if node: count+=1; if not last_level: queue.append(node.left); queue.append(node.right); if count==expected: if level==height-1: last_level=True; level+=1; expected*=2; count=0.

examples.trace: Start: queue=[1], level=0, count=0, expected=1, last_level=False. Process 1: non-null, count=1, add children 2,3. count==expected, level=0 not last (height-1=2), so level=1, expected=2, count=0. Queue now [2,3]. Level 1: process 2: non-null, count=1, add 4,5; process 3: non-null, count=2, add null,null. count==expected, level=1 not last, so level=2, expected=4, count=0, last_level set True. Queue now [4,5,null,null]. Level 2: process 4,5,null,null, count updates accordingly.

Start: queue=[1], level=0, count=0, expected=1. Process 1: count=1, add 2,3. Level complete, level=1, expected=2. Queue [2,3]. Level 1: process 2: count=1, add null,4; process 3: count=2, add null,null. Level complete, level=2, expected=4, last_level True. Queue [null,4,null,null]. Level 2: process null, then 4, etc.

ladders.title: Building Block — Validate 'No Gaps' in Last Level
ladders.desc: Check that in the last level of a tree, all non-null nodes are left-aligned with no gaps.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on validating the last level of a binary tree to ensure nodes are as far left as possible, which is a key property of complete trees. The intuition is that in a complete tree, the last level may not be full, but any nodes present must fill the level from left to right without holes. After performing BFS and identifying the last level, we need to verify that once we encounter a null node in this level, all subsequent nodes in the queue for this level must also be null. The algorithm: during BFS, when we're processing the last level, we enqueue children as usual but track if we've seen a null. Once a null is dequeued, set a flag 'gap_seen'. For any subsequent node in the same level, if it's non-null and gap_seen is true, return false (gap violation). Also, ensure we only check nodes at the last level depth. Time O(k) where k is nodes in last level, space O(1) extra. Connects to L2 by adding the last-level validation to the traversal. Common mistakes: not restricting check to last level only, incorrectly resetting gap flag, or not handling multiple nulls correctly. Real code pattern: In BFS loop for last level: if node is null: gap_seen=True; else: if gap_seen: return False.

examples.trace: Process 4: non-null, gap_seen=False. Process 5: non-null, gap_seen=False. Process null: gap_seen set True. Process null: gap_seen True, node null, okay. No violation, return true.
Process 4: non-null, gap_seen=False. Process null: gap_seen set True. Process 5: non-null, but gap_seen True, so violation. Return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Enqueue and Dequeue in BFS
ladders.desc: Implement the fundamental queue operations for level-order traversal.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a queue to perform breadth-first traversal of a binary tree. The intuition is that BFS requires visiting nodes level by level, which naturally uses a FIFO (First-In-First-Out) data structure. We enqueue nodes as we discover them (starting with root) and dequeue to process them in order. For each dequeued node, we process it (e.g., check value) and enqueue its children (left then right) to be processed later. This ensures nodes at depth d are processed before nodes at depth d+1. The algorithm: initialize empty queue; enqueue root; while queue not empty: dequeue node; if node not null: process node; enqueue node.left; enqueue node.right. Time O(n), space O(w) where w is maximum width. Connects to L3 by providing the mechanism to traverse levels. Common mistakes: enqueuing null nodes unnecessarily (wastes space but okay for simplicity), using stack instead of queue (gives DFS), or infinite loop if not checking queue emptiness. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; queue=deque(); queue.append(root); while queue: node=queue.popleft(); if node: print(node.val); queue.append(node.left); queue.append(node.right).

examples.trace: Start queue=[1]. Dequeue 1, process 1, enqueue 2,3. Queue=[2,3]. Dequeue 2, process 2, enqueue null,null. Queue=[3,null,null]. Dequeue 3, process 3, enqueue null,null. Queue=[null,null,null,null]. Continue until queue empty.

Queue=[1]. Dequeue 1, process 1, enqueue 2,null. Queue=[2,null]. Dequeue 2, process 2, enqueue 3,null. Queue=[null,3,null]. Dequeue null, skip. Dequeue 3, process 3, enqueue null,null. Queue=[null,null,null]. Dequeue nulls until empty.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Null Handling
ladders.desc: Understand the basic representation of a binary tree node and how to handle null children.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the fundamental building block of binary trees: the node structure. A binary tree node typically has three components: a value (e.g., integer), a left child reference, and a right child reference. In many languages, this is represented as a class or struct. The intuition is that trees are recursive structures where each node points to subtrees. Null (or None) represents the absence of a child, which is crucial for defining leaf nodes and tree boundaries. For example, a leaf node has both children null. When traversing or computing properties, we must always check for null to avoid errors. The basic operations include creating a node, accessing its value and children, and checking if a node is null. This connects to L4 as BFS requires accessing children which may be null. Common mistakes: not initializing children to null, dereferencing null pointers, or misunderstanding that null is a valid tree state (empty tree). Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right. To handle null: if node is None: return base_case.

examples.input: Create node with val=1, left=null, right=null
examples.trace: Initialize TreeNode with val=1. Set left attribute to None. Set right attribute to None. Result is a leaf node.

examples.input: Access children of node with left=2, right=3
examples.trace: Given node n, n.left returns reference to node with val 2, n.right returns reference to node with val 3. If children were null, accessing would return None.

testCases.input: Check if null node has children
Tree: root val=1, left val=2, right val=3
Tree where a node has only left child
